pages.chatInterface.activeFile.text: Enable "Auto-add active file context" in Settings → Interface to automatically include the note you're currently viewing in every message. A badge in the input area shows the filename. This lets you ask questions like "Summarize this note" or "Find related notes" without having to attach the file manually.
pages.chatInterface.activityBlock.list:
• Tool name in a monospace badge
  • Key parameters (e.g., the file path for read_file)
  • Diff badge for write operations: +N / -N lines changed
  • Result shown on expansion
pages.chatInterface.activityBlock.text1: When the agent is working, an expandable activity block appears below the spinner. It shows every tool call in real time — the tool name, its inputs, and the result (success or error). You can expand it at any time while the agent is still running.
pages.chatInterface.approvalFlow.callout: Tip Configure permanent auto-approval in Settings → Permissions to skip the approval card for operations you always trust (e.g., read operations are auto-approved by default).
pages.chatInterface.approvalFlow.list:
• Tool name and file path — what will change
  • Diff preview — added (green) and removed (red) lines for edits
  • Approve — execute the operation and continue
  • Reject — block this operation (the agent will be told it was rejected)
  • Auto-approve — approve this operation type for the rest of the session
pages.chatInterface.approvalFlow.text: When the agent wants to create, edit, or delete a file, an approval card appears:
pages.chatInterface.autocomplete.list:
• / — opens a list of your workflows and custom prompts. Select one to expand it as a message template. For example, type /daily-review to run your review workflow.
  • @ — opens the vault file picker to attach notes as context.
pages.chatInterface.autocomplete.text: Two shortcut triggers are available in the input:
pages.chatInterface.chatControls.list:
• New Chat (header button) — clears the conversation and starts fresh
  • Stop (appears while agent is running) — aborts the current request
  • Regenerate — re-sends the last user message, discarding the previous response
  • Save Chat — saves the current conversation to a JSON file in your vault (if chat history folder is configured)
pages.chatInterface.chatHistory.text1: Chat history is enabled by default. Every conversation is automatically saved and can be restored from the history panel. Click the history icon in the sidebar header to browse past conversations grouped by date.
pages.chatInterface.chatHistory.text2: For full details on chat history, memory, and personalization, see Memory & Chat History.
pages.chatInterface.fileAttachments.text: Type @ in the input to open the Vault File Picker — a live-search list of all your vault files. Select one (or several) to include their content as context. Attached files appear as chips in the input area and can be removed individually. This is useful when you want the agent to reference a specific note without switching to it.
pages.chatInterface.keyboard.table: ShortcutAction EnterSend message (default) Shift+EnterInsert newline Ctrl/Cmd+EnterAlternative send (when Enter-to-send is disabled) EscapeClose autocomplete popover @Open vault file picker /Open workflow/prompt autocomplete
pages.chatInterface.modelModeSwitcher.li:
• Mode selector (left) — switch between Ask, Agent, and custom modes. The switch takes effect for the next message.
  • Model selector (right) — switch the AI model. Shows all enabled models.
pages.chatInterface.modelModeSwitcher.te: The chat header has two selectors:
The agent can also switch modes mid-conversation with the switch_mode tool if the task calls for it (requires approval).
pages.chatInterface.overview.list:
• Header — mode selector, model selector, new chat button
  • Messages area — conversation history with real-time streaming
  • Input area — text input with toolbar (attachments, tools, send)
pages.chatInterface.overview.text: The sidebar has three main zones:
pages.chatInterface.sendingMessages.list:
• Enter — send the message (default)
  • Shift+Enter — insert a newline
  • Ctrl/Cmd+Enter — alternative send key (configurable in Settings → Interface)
pages.chatInterface.sendingMessages.text: Type your message in the input area at the bottom of the sidebar.
You can change the send key behavior in Settings → Interface → "Press Enter to send".
pages.chatInterface.subtitle: Everything you do in Obsilo starts in the sidebar. Here's how to get the most out of it — from sending your first message to attaching files, using autocomplete, and reading the activity log.
pages.chatInterface.title: Chat Interface
pages.chatInterface.todoList.text: When the agent calls update_todo_list to plan a complex task, a checklist card appears in the conversation. It updates live as the agent completes steps:
pages.chatInterface.tokenUsage.text: After each response, a token badge shows the cumulative input and output tokens used in the conversation. This helps you track costs and understand when context condensing might be useful.
pages.chatInterface.toolPicker.text: Click the wrench icon in the input toolbar to open the Tool Picker popover. This lets you temporarily override which tools, skills, or workflows are active for the current session — without changing your permanent settings.
pages.gettingStarted.addModel.table: ProviderWhere to get keyRecommended model Anthropicconsole.anthropic.comClaude Sonnet 4.5 OpenAIplatform.openai.comGPT-4o OpenRouteropenrouter.aiLlama 3.2 3B (free tier) OllamaRun locally: ollama servellama3.2 or qwen2.5:7b
pages.gettingStarted.addModel.text1: Open Obsidian Settings → Obsilo Agent → Providers → Models.
pages.gettingStarted.addModel.text2: You'll see pre-configured built-in models. Click Edit on any model to add your API key and enable it:
pages.gettingStarted.addModel.text3: After entering the key, click Test Connection to verify it works. Then enable the model to make it available in the chat.
pages.gettingStarted.brat.step1:
Install BRAT

In Obsidian: Settings → Community Plugins → Browse → search for "BRAT" → Install and Enable.
pages.gettingStarted.brat.step2:
Add Obsilo Agent

Open BRAT settings → Add Beta Plugin → paste:

https://github.com/pssah4/obsilo

Click Add Plugin. BRAT downloads and installs the latest release.
pages.gettingStarted.brat.step3:
Enable the plugin

Go to Settings → Community Plugins → scroll to "Obsilo Agent" → toggle on.
pages.gettingStarted.brat.text: BRAT (Beta Reviewers Auto-update Tool) lets you install Obsilo Agent directly from GitHub without building from source, and keeps it up to date automatically.
pages.gettingStarted.firstTask.list1:
• "What are my most recently modified notes?"
  • "Find all notes tagged with #project"
  • "What do I know about [any topic in your vault]?"
pages.gettingStarted.firstTask.list2:
• "Create a weekly review template with sections for goals, accomplishments, and next steps"
  • "Add a 'status' frontmatter field to all notes in my Projects folder"
  • "Research [topic] on the web and save a summary note to my Research folder"
pages.gettingStarted.firstTask.text1: The sidebar opens in Ask mode by default — a read-only assistant that answers questions about your vault without touching any files. Try:
pages.gettingStarted.firstTask.text2: Switch to Agent mode using the mode selector in the chat header when you want to create, edit, or reorganize. The agent will show you a diff and ask for your approval before changing any file. Try:
pages.gettingStarted.installation.callou: Development mode Run npm run dev to watch for changes and hot-deploy on every save. Set the target vault path in esbuild.config.mjs (VAULT_PLUGIN_DIR) to auto-deploy on each build.
pages.gettingStarted.intro.callout: What you'll be able to do Ask questions about your vault, create and edit notes with AI assistance, research topics on the web and save the results, organize notes in bulk — all from a sidebar inside Obsidian, with a diff preview and approval step before anything changes.
pages.gettingStarted.manual.step1:
Clone the repository 
git clone https://github.com/pssah4/obsilo.git cd obsidian-agent
pages.gettingStarted.manual.step2:
Install dependencies and build 
npm install npm run build
This produces main.js in the project root.
pages.gettingStarted.manual.step3:
Copy to your vault

Create the plugin directory and copy the three required files:

mkdir -p "/path/to/your-vault/.obsidian/plugins/obsilo-agent" cp main.js styles.css manifest.json \ "/path/to/your-vault/.obsidian/plugins/obsilo-agent/"
pages.gettingStarted.manual.step4:
Enable in Obsidian

Open Obsidian → Settings → Community Plugins → scroll to "Obsilo Agent" → toggle it on.

Note If you don't see the plugin, make sure "Safe Mode" is turned off in Community Plugins settings.
pages.gettingStarted.nextSteps.chatInter: Learn about the sidebar UI, attachments, autocomplete, and activity log.
pages.gettingStarted.nextSteps.modesDesc: Understand built-in modes and create your own custom agent personas.
pages.gettingStarted.nextSteps.permissio: Configure which operations need your approval and which are auto-approved.
pages.gettingStarted.nextSteps.rulesSkil: Customize how the agent behaves with permanent instructions and templates.
pages.gettingStarted.openSidebar.text1: Click the Obsilo Agent icon in the Obsidian ribbon (left or right edge of the window). If you don't see it, use the Command Palette (<kbd>Ctrl/Cmd+P</kbd>) and search for "Open Agent Sidebar".
pages.gettingStarted.openSidebar.text2: The sidebar defaults to the right panel. You can move it to the left in Settings → Interface → Sidebar Position.
pages.gettingStarted.requirements.table: ObsidianVersion 1.4.0 or later PlatformDesktop only (macOS, Windows, Linux). Not available on mobile. Node.js18 or later (for building from source) API KeyAnthropic, OpenAI, OpenRouter, or a running Ollama instance
pages.gettingStarted.semanticSearchSetup: 4. Set Up Semantic Search (Optional but Recommended)
Go to Settings → Obsilo Agent → Semantic Index
Choose an embedding model. The default Xenova/all-MiniLM-L6-v2 runs locally and requires no API key
Click Build Index and wait for indexing to complete (the first build may take a few minutes)
Semantic search lets the agent find notes by meaning rather than just keywords. It runs entirely locally — no cloud service needed.
Once built, the semantic_search tool is available to the agent in all modes.
pages.gettingStarted.subtitle: Obsilo is in early beta and open for testing. It has not been published as Community Plugin yet, hence you'll need to use one of the two other options described below. Install Obsilo Agent, connect your first AI model, and run your first task in under 5 minutes.
pages.gettingStarted.title: Getting Started
pages.memory.autoTitle.text: After the first assistant response, Obsilo generates a short title (3-8 words) for the conversation using the active model. This title appears in the history panel and helps you find conversations later.
pages.memory.chatHistory.text: Chat history is enabled by default. Every conversation is saved automatically as a JSON file inside the plugin directory (.obsidian/plugins/obsilo-agent/history/).
pages.memory.historyPanel.list:
• Today / Yesterday / This Week / Older
  • Each entry shows the conversation title, date, and message count
  • Use the text filter at the top to search through titles
  • Hover over an entry to reveal the delete button
pages.memory.historyPanel.text: Click the history icon in the sidebar header (left of the New Chat button) to open the history panel. It slides in from the left and shows your conversations grouped by date:
pages.memory.howItWorks.callout: Zero impact on chat speed Memory extraction runs entirely in the background after the conversation ends. It never blocks or slows down your active conversation. If Obsidian closes before extraction finishes, the queue is processed on next startup.
pages.memory.howItWorks.list:
• You have a conversation with the agent as usual
  • When the conversation ends (new chat or close), if it had enough messages (configurable, default: 6), a background extraction job is queued
  • The extraction model (a separate, cheap model you configure) processes the queue:
    • First, it creates a session summary (key topics, decisions, preferences observed)
    • Then, it checks if any new facts should be promoted to long-term memory (your profile, projects, or patterns)
  • Next time you start a conversation, the agent has your profile, projects, and patterns in its system prompt — plus relevant session summaries retrieved via semantic search
pages.memory.memoryFiles.table: FileContainsLoaded into context user-profile.mdYour name, language, communication style, agent preferencesAlways (system prompt) projects.mdActive projects, goals, tech stackAlways (system prompt) patterns.mdBehavioral patterns, common refinements, workflow habitsAlways (system prompt) knowledge.mdDomain expertise, specialized knowledgeOn demand (semantic search) sessions/*.mdPer-conversation summaries with YAML frontmatterOn demand (semantic search)
pages.memory.memoryFiles.text: Memory is stored as editable Markdown files in .obsidian/plugins/obsilo-agent/memory/:
pages.memory.memoryFiles.text2: You can edit these files directly — they are plain Markdown. Changes take effect on the next conversation.
pages.memory.memorySystem.list:
• Session memory — a summary of each conversation, stored as a Markdown file
  • Long-term memory — your profile, active projects, and behavioral patterns, distilled from session summaries
pages.memory.memorySystem.text: The memory system has two layers:
pages.memory.onboarding.text1: The first time you use Obsilo (when no user profile exists), the agent starts an onboarding conversation. It naturally asks about your name, language preference, vault usage, and communication style. The answers are extracted into your user profile after the conversation ends.
pages.memory.onboarding.text2: You can re-run onboarding at any time from Settings → Memory → Re-run Onboarding.
pages.memory.privacyCallout: Privacy first All memory data is stored locally in your plugin directory. Memory extraction uses a dedicated model you choose (ideally a small, cheap model like Haiku). No data leaves your machine except for the LLM API calls you configure.
pages.memory.restore.text: Click any conversation in the history panel to restore it. The full message history is loaded back into the sidebar — including assistant messages, tool calls, and the internal conversation state. You can continue the conversation exactly where you left off.
pages.memory.sessionContext.text: When you start a new conversation, Obsilo uses your first message to semantically search past session summaries. The top 3 most relevant summaries are injected into the system prompt as context. This means the agent can reference previous conversations when relevant — without you having to remind it.
pages.memory.settings.callout: Tip You must select a memory model before extraction works. If no model is selected, conversations are saved to history but no memory extraction runs.
pages.memory.settings.table: SettingDefaultDescription Enable memoryOnMaster toggle for the memory system Auto-extract sessionsOnAutomatically create session summaries when conversations end Auto-update long-termOnPromote new facts from sessions to long-term memory files Memory model(none)Dropdown to pick the model for extraction. Choose a small, fast model (e.g., Haiku) for cost efficiency Extraction threshold6Minimum message count before a conversation triggers extraction (slider: 2-20) View memory files-Opens the memory directory in Obsidian Reset all memory-Deletes all memory files (with confirmation)
pages.memory.settings.text: All memory settings are under Settings → Agent Behaviour → Memory:
pages.memory.subtitle: Obsilo remembers your conversations and learns your preferences over time. Chat history stores every conversation for browsing and restore. The memory system automatically extracts knowledge from your conversations and personalizes the agent across sessions.
pages.memory.title: Memory & Chat History
